items.q: Are spoken fillers such as 'um' and 'you know' always a sign of poor speaking?
items.correct: No; they are a natural feature of spontaneous speech and can give thinking time, though too many can distract.
items.misconception: They always mean the speaker is unprepared and a poor communicator.
items.why: Students judge natural spontaneous speech features by the standards of written English.

items.q: Is formal Standard English always the best choice when speaking?
items.correct: No; the right register depends on audience and purpose, so informal speech may suit a casual context.
items.misconception: Formal Standard English is correct in every speaking situation.
items.random_wrong: You should never use Standard English when speaking.
items.why: Learners assume one register fits all, ignoring that appropriateness depends on context.

items.q: Does good speaking depend only on the words you say?
items.correct: No; non-verbal communication such as eye contact, tone and gesture is also vital to delivery.
items.misconception: Only the words matter; body language is irrelevant.
items.random_wrong: Speaking well depends only on how loudly you talk.
items.why: Students overlook the role of non-verbal communication in effective spoken delivery.

items.q: Adjusting your vocabulary and tone to suit your listeners is known as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Audience awareness. “Proofreading” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Eye contact, gesture and facial expression are forms of:
items.why: It's Non-verbal communication. “Standard spelling” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In a formal presentation to an examiner, the most appropriate register is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Formal Standard English. “Slang and abbreviations” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Listening attentively and responding to what others say in a discussion is called:
items.why: It's Active listening. “Skimming” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
